title: The Collapse of Federal Funding for Non-profits
author: Daniel Stid
text:
In The Sun Also Rises, Scottish veteran Mike Campbell is asked how he went bankrupt. “Two ways,” he says. “Gradually and then suddenly.” When asked what brought it on, he replies, “Friends. I had a lot of friends. False friends. Then I had creditors, too.”

This rueful retrospection from a Hemingway character all too aptly describes the current state of federal funding for non-profit organizations. Starting in the mid-1960s, the federal government increasingly relied on and funded non-profit service providers to implement a range of policies and programs that Congress had passed. The government has supported these non-profits directly, through federal grants and contracts, and indirectly, through funding streams that pass through state and local government. Now, this hybrid system of federally funded, non-profit-provided services has collapsed.

Donald Trump’s second administration routinely expresses hostility toward non-profits that have come to rely on federal funding. It declared early on an intent to “stop funding NGOs that undermine the national interest.” The administration quickly started making good on this vow with grant reviews, cancelations, spending freezes, impoundments, and rescissions. Tens of thousands of non-profit employees have already lost their jobs. The spending cuts enacted in the One Big Beautiful Bill will further crimp federal funding for non-profits, even while increasing demand for their services.

That said, we cannot attribute the hybrid system’s demise solely to the tornado that is the second Trump administration. Its sudden toppling resulted from mounting contradictions that ate away at its foundations and legitimacy for decades, like so many termites. In light of this, we should not try to salvage the old hybrid system. But we can reimagine it — and now have little choice but to do so.

THE RISE OF THE HYBRID SYSTEM

The public-private system of service provision originated with liberal reformers in the early 1960s. The Ford Foundation piloted several programs that used community-based non-profits to deliver services and benefits to urban residents. Democratic presidents John Kennedy and (especially) Lyndon Johnson sought to embed these approaches into federal policies. They formed the backbone of Johnson’s War on Poverty and his expansive push for a Great Society through initiatives like Head Start, VISTA, Job Corps, and the Community Action Program.

Critics of the current administration’s funding cuts and impoundments perceive them as merely the latest attack in the right’s ongoing campaign against federal support for people in need. But it is worth noting that, 50 years ago, conservatives stood in the vanguard of those championing the hybrid system then emerging.

The seminal text here is a 1977 pamphlet entitled “To Empower People: From State to Civil Society” by Peter Berger, a sociologist, and Richard John Neuhaus, then a Lutheran pastor. They published it under the auspices of the American Enterprise Institute. The authors began with the observation that

Two seemingly contradictory tendencies are evident in current thinking about public policy in America. First, there is a continuing desire for the services provided by the modern welfare state….The second tendency is one of strong animus against government, bureaucracy, and bigness as such.

Berger and Neuhaus held that the associations of civil society provided the best means of reconciling these opposing tendencies. They focused on four types of associations in particular: neighborhoods, families, religious congregations, and voluntary groups. They served as society’s essential “mediating structures,” or “institutions standing between the individual in his private life and the large institutions of public life.”

Berger and Neuhaus distilled their argument into three propositions. The first, their core premise, was that “mediating structures are essential for a vital democratic society.” The other two propositions were recommendations that followed from this premise. At a minimum, they argued, “public policy should protect and foster mediating structures.” More ambitiously, “wherever possible, public policy should utilize mediating structures for the realization of social purposes.”

This last “maximalist” proposition was, as the authors knew, their most controversial. They contended not only that religious congregations and voluntary associations should be left free to pursue their missions in the name of pluralism, but also that the federal government could enlist and fund these mediating structures to carry out public policies.

If all went well, Americans would receive the public services that many had come to expect and rely on without experiencing the impersonal and remote bureaucracy of government provision. It would be a fraught experiment, to be sure, one that could co-opt mediating structures and government institutions alike, undermining their legitimacy. But the potential benefits made it a risk worth taking.

MOYNIHAN’S EARLY WARNING

The cautious optimism that small “c” conservatives like Berger and Neuhaus harbored for the intermingling of the government and charitable sectors contrasted with the deeper-seated misgivings of Democratic senator Daniel Patrick Moynihan. While not a card-carrying neoconservative, Moynihan had been disillusioned if not mugged by reality well before his election to the Senate in 1976.

As a wonkish young aide in the Kennedy and Johnson administrations, Moynihan had helped draft the Economic Opportunity Act of 1964. This landmark bill came to serve as the main thrust of Johnson’s War on Poverty. Moynihan subsequently witnessed with chagrin how a throwaway line in the Community Action Program included in this omnibus legislation came to confound its implementation.

The provision called for “maximum feasible participation” by community members in the War on Poverty. The “maximum feasible misunderstanding” that resulted, to borrow the titular phrase from Moynihan’s book on the subject, undermined the authority of local governments and community institutions while leaving the intended beneficiaries of the Great Society with even more glaring unmet needs. Rather than attending to nuance in local contexts, this top-down federal intervention trampled all over it.

No doubt this searing experience was in Moynihan’s mind as he gave a speech at the Charter Meeting of the Independent Sector, a new membership association for non-profits and foundations, on March 5, 1980. Moynihan’s keynote was at once celebratory and bracing. After emphasizing and paying homage to the “alternative modes of common provision and public enterprise” the sector represented, he went on to tell the non-profit and philanthropic leaders in his audience that, “you are necessary, you are important and you are in some jeopardy.”

The threat was a swelling, imperious federal government then encroaching on their autonomy, which the Independent Sector had been set up to preserve and enhance. “Little by little,” he observed, “you are being squeezed out of existence or slowly absorbed,” becoming vassals to their government paymasters. Moynihan remarked how astonished he was to have learned recently that more than half of Catholic Charities’ revenue was then coming from government sources:

In time, there cannot be any outcome to that encroachment save government control. We have two possibilities: One is the disappearance of the independent sector, or — just as powerful a possibility — its subversion, so that it only appears to continue.

Not wishing to leave his audience despondent, Moynihan resolved in his peroration that “we don’t want that and we don’t have to have it.” He reassured his audience that they had friends on both sides of the aisle in Congress, not least himself, dedicated to working against his dire predictions. But he left no doubt that real pitfalls lay ahead.

AN OSTENSIBLY INDEPENDENT SECTOR

Over the next 12 years, the administrations of Ronald Reagan and George H. W. Bush furthered the federal government’s reliance on non-profits to implement its domestic policies via grants and contracts. More of this federal funding for non-profits flowed indirectly, through state- and local-government recipients of augmented block grants. These trends helped realize the downsized federal bureaucracy Reagan emphasized as well as Bush’s turn toward “a thousand points of light” in civil society.

In 1993, political scientists Steven Smith and Michael Lipsky took stock of the accumulating changes in Nonprofits for Hire: The Welfare State in the Age of Contracting. Lipsky was then a program officer for the Ford Foundation, which three decades earlier had first spurred the government to rely more on non-profits in the administration of social policy, so his perspective was an especially telling one. Smith and Lipsky found that

[t]he service sector and its nonprofit providers have been transformed under the subtle pressures of the contracting regime…[nonprofit managers] must now hustle for contracts, compete with other agencies for scarce resources and bend their priorities to meet the new demands of their government counterparts.

The authors proceeded to pose and explore a fundamental question and conundrum:

If the state no longer directly delivers services but authorizes private parties to conduct its business, where shall we locate the boundaries of the state?…Unlike the starry image President Bush put before the public, more dependence on nonprofit organizations means not less but more government involvement in the affairs of voluntary and community agencies.

Smith and Lipsky traced how the contracting regime masked ongoing growth in the scale and scope of government activity. This system also increasingly led non-profits to become less voluntary and responsive and more professionalized and bureaucratized. More and more, non-profit service providers effectively functioned as agents of state authority vis-a-vis the people and communities they served. These trends threatened to undermine the legitimacy of both government agencies and their non-profit counterparts.

During the 1990s, the Clinton administration’s push — personally spearheaded by Vice President Al Gore — to “reinvent government” accelerated federal reliance on mission-driven non-profits to provide human and social services. Through such tools as performance-based contracting, the presumption was that they could do so with greater effectiveness and accountability — and at lower cost.

Not everyone was enamored with the idea. In 1996, after 15 years at the helm of the Independent Sector, Brian O’Connell sought to pour cold water on the renewed enthusiasm for outsourcing government service provision to non-profits. He did so in an essay for the Public Administration Review, where he underscored three big problems with the growing reliance of the federal government on non-profits to carry out its policies.

The first was that it glossed over the obligation that the government had to provide basic and essential services to its citizens: “Though the activity can be delegated, the responsibility cannot.”

Second, it distracted voluntary associations by pulling them increasingly into the work of service provision, where a growing pot of resources could help them meet their budget needs. But in O’Connell’s estimation, the increased funding for service provision came at too great an opportunity cost. It preoccupied non-profits, shifting their focus away from what he contended were higher callings: advocacy and citizen empowerment.

Third, echoing Moynihan’s earlier warning, O’Connell issued a strong caveat to members of an ostensibly independent sector:

My experience tells me that long-term government support of an agency or complex of agencies creates a quasi-governmental entity with decreasing value as an independent force. By no means does this suggest that voluntary organizations that receive substantial government funding cannot still be effective representatives of their clients and causes, but if government is a very substantial source for an organization’s support, its ability to be relied upon as an independent force has to be in question.

“TO EMPOWER PEOPLE” REVISITED

The mid-1990s brought a sea change in the politics and policies of the federal government — one that expanded the developments that concerned O’Connell.

In the 1994 elections, for the first time in four decades, Republicans gained control of both houses of Congress and set out to reduce government spending, cut taxes, and reform welfare. Subsequently, in his 1996 State of the Union Address, Bill Clinton bowed to the GOP agenda, declaring that “the era of big government is over.” Over the next several years, he struck landmark compromises with emboldened GOP majorities to reform welfare and balance the federal budget.

During this heady period, Peter Berger and Richard John Neuhaus revisited the arguments they had proffered back in the 1970s. In many respects, their mediating-structures framework had provided the blueprint for the federal government’s domestic-policy agenda over the subsequent two decades. Thus, their retrospection was something of a victory lap. Yet their reassessment included difficult and even dark lines of self-criticism.

On the one hand, Berger and Neuhaus appreciated that they now had the wind in their sails: “[T]he political climate today,” they wrote, “is much more auspicious for realizing at least some of the practical implications of the [mediating structures] concept.” On the other hand, the authors flagged a problem that “has increasingly come to preoccupy us in the intervening years: how to protect mediating structures from the fatal embrace of governmental regulation.” They went on to lament:

We were aware of this problem at the time, but we would now say that we did not take it seriously enough. Arguably, it has greatly worsened since then. Unless that problem is solved, when such institutions are first “discovered” and then funded by government, the very vitality that originally distinguished the institutions from government agencies is destroyed. Indeed they become government agencies under another name. This is not just an eventuality. It has already happened, across the board.

Two problems lay downstream of this one. First, it was not clear how to protect, let alone liberate, government-funded non-profits from this corrupting influence. It had led many such institutions to “reshape themselves to continue as beneficiaries of government largesse,” and they in turn had become “quite content with their status as parastatal agencies.”

Second, to secure public funding, non-profit service providers that were religious had “been forced to deemphasize or even deny altogether the religious character that originally served as their raison d’être.” Unable to draw on their transcendent purposes, they were reduced to meeting merely material needs alongside all the other contractors.

The 1996 reappraisal by Berger and Neuhaus was the centerpiece in a symposium of essays by think tankers, academics, and civil-society leaders. They all reflected on how the mediating-structures hypothesis had held up over the years. Michael Joyce and William Schambra, then serving as the president and director of programs at the Bradley Foundation, respectively, co-authored an especially telling and prescient assessment.

Joyce and Schambra began by declaring the exhaustion if not the death of “a grand political project for America.” This endeavor had been driven by elites seeking to build “a great national community, which would summon Americans away from selfish interests and parochial allegiances toward a commitment to an overarching national purpose.” This project started back in the progressive era at the turn of the 20th century and reached its high-water mark with Johnson’s War on Poverty.

Indeed, the Great Society probably came as close as any other effort in the twentieth century to capturing progressivism’s ideal: public policy securely in the hands of an elite cadre of professionals, dispensing programs through vast, gleaming rational bureaucracies.

Joyce and Schambra contended that the national progressive project, burdened by the weight of its own pretensions, had failed to realize its objectives. In the process, it had corroded the more prosaic but sturdy forms of citizenship that had long prospered in local communities and the civic associations that had nurtured them.

Rather than humanizing and contextualizing federal policy by implementing it through mediating structures, the influence had predominantly run in the other direction. The federal government had turned these civic entities into de facto appendages of the administrative state. No wonder things looked bleak to Joyce and Schambra:

With the moral foundations of the liberal project thus eroding, its programmatic superstructure — a massive, centralized federal government — is left in a peculiarly exposed and precarious position. No longer understood to be the instrument of high national purpose, the federal government comes to be seen instead as a distant, alienating, bureaucratic monstrosity.

Looking back on this judgment from 1996, one can detect early but clear signals of populist frustration with a federal government controlled by elites and technocrats in Washington, D.C. Over the next three decades, this unrest would come to a roaring boil.

THE RISE AND FALL OF COMPASSIONATE CONSERVATISM

In the meantime, however, a new conservative administration sought to breathe new life and legitimacy into the hybrid model. During the 2000 campaign, George W. Bush vowed that his administration would combine compassion and conservatism to better realize the promise of both. As he put it in his stump speech, “[it] is compassionate to actively help our fellow citizens in need. It is conservative to insist on responsibility and results. And with this hopeful approach, we will make a real difference in people’s lives.”

Bush’s aspiration informed several landmark bipartisan achievements of his presidency, including the No Child Left Behind Act to reform K-12 education and the President’s Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief (PEPFAR), which focused on sub-Saharan Africa. That said, at the outset of his presidency, revitalizing federally funded, non-profit-delivered services in the United States was the primary aim of Bush’s compassionate conservatism.

The theory behind this agenda was perhaps most cogently developed and operationalized by John J. DiIulio, Jr., a scholar of public administration at the University of Pennsylvania. He summarized it in a 1999 essay for the Weekly Standard:

Compassionate conservatism has the makings of a coherent public philosophy, one that is entirely consonant with the Founding Fathers’ understanding of civil society and government, and with most contemporary Americans’ understanding as well.

The crux of compassionate conservatism is the will to promote active benevolence in all sectors of civil society and to institute results-driven competition within social-welfare bureaucracies, federal, state, and local. If implemented faithfully, the policies and programs that flow from compassionate conservatism might bring lasting progress against our worst social ills — notably, enormous improvements in how government helps poor children and dysfunctional families.

Subsequently, at the outset of his term, Bush named DiIulio the inaugural director of the Office of Faith-Based and Community Initiatives (OFBCI), which the president created to advance the compassionate-conservatism agenda.

DiIulio and his team prioritized enabling community-based religious non-profits to compete for government grants and contracts on an equal footing with often much larger secular non-profits. OFBCI’s working hypothesis — to be tested with data — was that these smaller community- and faith-based organizations had untapped potential to help solve seemingly intractable social problems. A quick review conducted by OFBCI suggested that, despite several bipartisan “charitable choice” laws passed during Clinton’s second term to help achieve this goal, local faith-based organizations still lacked equal access to federal funding. As DiIulio would later observe about his time and mission in government,

Establishing a level playing field for receiving federal funds was both desirable in its own right and a necessary but insufficient condition for achieving the primary, people-helping task….It would be a nice bonus if, after shaking up the government-by-proxy system with new faith-based grantees, federal programs started solving social problems. If this were to happen on a large scale, it could deepen and widen political support for antipoverty and related civic initiatives.

Unfortunately, this hopeful effort to revamp the hybrid system quickly ran into obstacles. The acrimonious fallout from the contested 2000 election cut against the bipartisanship that would have been key to the effort’s success. A cadre of White House political operatives, more zealous Republicans in Congress, and evangelicals sought to use faith-based initiatives to beat up on Democrats. For their part, more partisan Democrats bucked against giving Bush a signature policy win in this area. Then, after 9/11, the focus quickly shifted to countering terrorism and waging wars abroad. The window for conservative reform, open all too briefly, had closed.

THE SOCIAL-INNOVATION AGENDA

Barack Obama was not prepared to write off the hybrid model. His administration sought to refresh the system with social and financial innovations in support of a bold vision: The federal government would scale up and pay for what works — and stop paying for what does not.

Ashoka vice president Michele Jolin helped cast this vision with an influential 2008 article in the Stanford Social Innovation Review titled, “Innovating the White House: How the next president of the United States can spur social entrepreneurship.” Writing during the presidential primary campaign, Jolin held:

Since the 1960s, the nonprofit sector has served as a test lab for what works in government at all levels; the federal government even adapted a number of successful nonprofit approaches into full-scale programs (AmeriCorps and YouthBuild).

To tackle the problems of the scale and scope our nation faces, however, the next president needs to focus less on replicating individual programs and more on reorienting the government’s relationship toward the nonprofit sector. To do this, he or she should focus on creating a policy environment that fosters greater social innovation and invests in the most high-impact social entrepreneurial efforts.

Jolin proposed a new White House office to help the new president carry out this agenda. Her sketch soon became reality. In 2009, Obama set up a White House Office of Social Innovation and Civic Participation, with Jolin serving initially as a senior advisor. Over the next several years, the administration launched several evidence-based policy initiatives with the goal, as the office’s theory of change subsequently put it, to “Find what works,” “Fund what works,” and “Build the enabling infrastructure.”

The administration’s approach drew on the logic of tiered-evidence models in which promising, early-stage experiments receive seed funding; those that demonstrate initial results receive more funding and further testing; and those shown to be effective by the highest standards of evidence receive funding to scale at the national level.

The Obama administration’s evidence-based initiatives included the Social Innovation Fund, housed at the Corporation for National and Community Service; the Investing in Innovation (i3) fund at the Department of Education; the Maternal, Infant, and Early Childhood Home Visiting and Teen Pregnancy Prevention programs at the Department of Health and Human Services; and the Workforce Innovation Fund at the Department of Labor.

Obama’s social-innovation agenda also rested heavily on the idea of public-private partnerships, especially when it came to funding what works. As the president put it in June 2009 remarks at a White House event to launch this push:

Our non-profits can provide the solutions. Our government can rigorously evaluate these solutions and invest limited taxpayer dollars in ones that work. But we need those of you from the private sector to step up, as well. We need you to provide that critical seed capital to launch these ideas. We need you to provide those matching funds to help them grow.

This logic, that government should only invest in programs that demonstrably work, also led the administration to back “pay for success” schemes, also known as “social-impact bonds.” Under this model, philanthropy or private capital would pay up front for the cost of a non-profit’s delivering public services to a given population. They would later be reimbursed by the government — but only if the non-profit hit the target outcomes.

Looking back, both the Obama-administration leaders and the philanthropists that gathered for the 2009 White House event may have left having heard what they wanted to believe. Obama and his aides may have presumed that their philanthropic guests were prepared to take on the lion’s share of the funding burden for the work intended, and those philanthropic guests might have assumed the same about the government. But neither side was really in a position to play the role envisioned by the other on a sustained basis.

To be sure, for the duration of the Obama years, the push to find and fund evidence-based policies and the non-profits implementing them pressed ahead. But these innovations came at the margins of the federal budget, most of which continued to flow through programs and policies not subject to the same degree of scrutiny. Then, with a few programmatic exceptions, the social-innovation agenda was discontinued by the first Trump administration.

THE SOCIAL-SERVICES INDUSTRIAL COMPLEX

The successive efforts by the Bush and Obama administrations to reset our hybrid system fell short for several reasons.

First, they assumed that not only federal but also state and local administrators and bureaucrats would be creative, nimble, data driven, and open to taking calculated risks. These general dispositions proved elusive at the scale required, especially among the procurement officials and grant-program managers who ultimately needed to operationalize the new approach. As a result, despite all the hopes many advocates and funders initially placed in paying for success, the skeptics turned out to be right.

There are, to be sure, many dedicated government leaders and civil servants with the requisite qualities. But these exceptions unfortunately prove the rule in a massive and complex system of bureaucracies that tends to reward rule-following, turf guarding, and stability over change.

Second, in grounding their proposed shifts on demonstrated evidence of results from the policies, programs, and non-profits delivering them, advocates subjected their plans to what Peter Rossi termed in 1987 “the iron law of evaluation.” It holds that the “expected value of any net impact assessment of any large scale social program is zero…i.e., that the program will have no effect.”

This “iron law” had been borne out across a wave of evaluations on the impact of federal social programs, several of which ran all the way back to Johnson’s War on Poverty. As Brookings Institution scholars noted in 2010:

Since 1990, there have been 10 instances in which an entire federal social program has been evaluated using the scientific “gold standard” method of randomly assigning individuals to a program or control group. Nine of these evaluations found weak or no positive effects.

If hallowed programs like Head Start and Job Corps had failed to deliver positive impact, it called into question whether more nascent programs could yield results if the federal government adopted them at a national scale.

But they never really got the chance, which indicates a third problem: These reform efforts, for all of their promise, remained minor eddies and tributaries relative to the massive rivers of federal funds that continued to flow to and through federal agencies, programs, and non-profit service providers. These entities had come to form what I called in 2012 — provocatively but, in retrospect, accurately — ”the social services industrial complex.” The inertia built into existing funding flows had, over decades, grown exceedingly difficult to shift, not least because of incumbent non-profits’ scale, contracting savvy, and political influence.

Participants in this complex have fought efforts by well-intentioned presidents and reformers to recast the system. Ultimately, however, the complex resisted change at its own peril. Its self-defense mechanisms have proven brittle in the face of disruptive political leaders and administrators driven by less-constructive intentions, who do not care about the purposes the system was originally meant to serve.

This raises the final and biggest problem: It would have taken a sustained bipartisan commitment over multiple administrations for the efforts described above to transform the hybrid service-delivery system. Had Jeb Bush won the 2016 election, spent two successful terms implementing a blend of compassionate conservatism and social innovation, and handed over the keys of the federal government to newly elected president Josh Shapiro, this hypothetical transformation could have taken root. But Donald Trump won the 2016 election, and then in 2024 was reelected to a second term, obliterating the prospect of an inside-the-system transformation.

LEVIATHAN BY PROXY

The critiques of the non-profit-government partnership surveyed thus far have focused on problems and unanticipated consequences within the system. But two more recent lines of criticism zero in on problems this system generates in our democracy.

The first arises from the conservative Madisonian perspective of John DiIulio. In a spirited 2014 polemic titled Bring Back the Bureaucrats: Why More Federal Workers Will Lead to Better (and Smaller!) Government (which had begun as an essay in these pages two years earlier), DiIulio took as his point of departure a remarkable discrepancy: While the federal government’s budget had (adjusting for inflation) increased by a factor of five since 1960, its civilian workforce over that same period had remained more or less constant at approximately 2 million.

Though elected officials in both parties pride themselves on keeping bureaucracy and big government at bay, DiIulio argued, they have in fact created a “Leviathan by Proxy.” Government in America is not in fact that small. When factoring in all its levels, our system of government now borrows and spends a similar amount per capita as its ostensibly more statist European peers.

But what Americans get from their big government is much spottier, primarily — this was DiIulio’s key point — because elected officials have outsourced the administration of federal policy to proxies beyond the national government, most notably to for-profit contractors and the non-profits it funds through contracts and grants. This has enabled politicians to pose as enemies of bureaucracy even as they create more diffuse, unaccountable, and thus pernicious forms of it.

DiIulio has sharpened this critique over the past decade. In 2023, he warned that “[t]here is a deep state, but it doesn’t emanate from within the federal bureaucracy. The real deep state is the contractor state” (his emphasis), which “uses federal bureaucrats the way sophisticated criminal syndicates use low-level bagmen.” Many large non-profits, he observed, are implicated in this scheme:

The real deep state’s heaviest money bags consistently go to mega-corporations via the U.S. Department of Defense. But big nonprofit organizations, including hospitals and universities that pay their executives millions of dollars a year in salaries, benefits, and deferred compensation, also get to drench their beaks.

For instance, in addition to billions of dollars a year in tax-free property and tax-deductible donations, nonprofits registered with the I.R.S. receive about 30 percent of their more than $2 trillion a year in revenues from government grants plus fees for goods and services from government.

DiIulio maintains that Leviathan by proxy confounds democracy in America from the top down. In her 2022 book Nonprofit Neighborhoods, the University of Maryland’s Claire Dunning explored how this same system has undermined democracy, as well as movements for greater economic and racial equality, from the bottom up.

Dunning focused on how a series of federal urban policies, beginning with the Johnson administration’s War on Poverty, rippled through American cities. To ground her analysis, she followed these efforts as they played out over decades in the “nonprofit neighborhoods” of Boston. Dunning defined these as “places where neighborhood-based nonprofit organizations controlled access to the levers of political, economic, and social power.” She also noted that, with time, they “became spaces of simultaneous inclusion and exclusion, where precarity was more often replicated than reduced.”

Dunning showed that the growth of urban non-profits in these neighborhoods was “a deliberate goal by Washington bureaucrats and not merely a secondary consequence.” This was because

[p]olicy makers recognized grant funding as both a carrot to draw private entities into the work of national programs and as a stick to discipline how they did so. Through this governing tool of incentive and enforcement, Washington helped build — and shape in its desired image — entire networks of community agencies in cities and rural areas around the country.

Dunning highlighted a persistent problem with the system of non-profit neighborhoods: It channeled and mediated the voices and interests of residents in ways that muffled direct challenges to the distribution of power within their communities. Where neighborhood non-profits have depended on government for their core funding, they have tended to divert and constrain “the political power of residents to make the kind of structural, policy-based changes necessary to win full rights of citizenship.”

Moreover, these same non-profits have often had to turn to wealthy philanthropists whenever government funding does not meet their needs, adding yet another set of external elite preferences to the mix. In summing up her assessment, Dunning underscored what she rightly called an “uncomfortable fact,” namely

that inequality and the role of neighborhood nonprofits in urban governance have grown together, not apart. In Boston, the city’s most economically and socially distressed neighborhoods in the twenty-first century are also those with the most neighborhood nonprofits.

From both the top down and the bottom up, from carefully reasoned and historically grounded perspectives on the Madisonian right and progressive left, the public-private system of service provision appears to be co-opting if not corrupting democracy in America.

It is no surprise, then, that the system finds itself under attack from an emboldened and unabashedly populist administration determined to strip away the institutional power of elites at all levels of American society. Nor is it a surprise that those defending the status quo ante are not broadly mobilized swaths of American citizens but rather a narrower set of “producer interests” working in or representing the non-profits affected by the administration’s actions.

WHAT COMES NEXT?

So long as the MAGA faction dominates the GOP, the consensus needed at the federal level to reconstruct the hybrid system is a non-starter. Moreover, with the fiscal imbalances facing the federal government — mounting interest payments on huge deficits as far as we can see, and looming shortfalls in funding for Social Security and Medicare — the resources for any such renewal are scarce. And given all the problems with the hybrid system, we should not attempt a restoration.

However, accepting the demise of the old system does not mean we should sit on our hands. The individuals, families, and communities the system was meant to help are no less in need, and many face ever more troubled circumstances. Those of us who seek more effective, sustainable, and legitimate ways of helping them have a lot of work to do. We can begin by anticipating some tangible changes and normative shifts that could help form a new, more resilient system.

First, non-profits should move away from depending on the now unreliable federal government and work more with state and local governments when it comes to public funding of social services. This will entail widespread experimentation and variation across the many laboratories of democracy in America. Some efforts will fall short; others will succeed. We can learn from both outcomes.

Along these lines, non-profits should pursue more holistic and neighborhood-based solutions. Federal programs tend to target individuals or families in need, zeroing in on one problem at a time. But individuals and families often have many needs at once, and many of them are best met through flourishing communities. While the current upheaval in our non-profit system creates tension points, it also frees up non-profits and state and local governments to adopt sensible new approaches, like those that Johns Hopkins University’s Seth Kaplan recommended in his book Fragile Neighborhoods.

Individual and foundation funders can also reallocate support away from advocacy for federal policies and funding that are not likely to bear fruit. Instead, they can prioritize grants to underwrite effective direct services on problems where the federal government is retrenching. Over the life of the hybrid system, old-fashioned charity became increasingly passé, and “systems change” strategies came into vogue among philanthropists. The pendulum is now swinging back.

The public-private system has long masked what services our government provides to people in need, why they do so, and who is responsible for delivering them. This has limited support for these government programs and their non-profit providers alike. We will need more clarity on both of these fronts in the years ahead.

To clarify different organizations’ roles, we may also need new legal-entity formats that better reflect how non-profits primarily funded by the government operate. Such entities are not government agencies, for-profit contractors, or privately funded non-profits. This leaves room to grant them status and affordances similar to other special entities, like charter schools, so they can flourish in their roles.

Social-service non-profits support tens of millions of Americans in need. These organizations’ role in our society should not be exploited as a political cudgel. Setting our system on the correct course will require changes that are creative and varied enough to confront the shortcomings that weighed down America’s hybrid setup. The goal should be to build a new, better system — not to patch together the vestiges of our old one. We should avoid the trap of thinking we simply need to rewind and repair the disruptions of the past year or two; the problems we ultimately need to solve have been accumulating for decades.
